POSIX Pipes  
Inter-Process Communication (IPC) using  
Anonymous Pipes

What Is a Pipe?  
A unidirectional communication channel between processes  
• Data flows like a queue: first-in, first-out (FIFO)  
• Created using pipe() system call  
• Exists in memory (kernel buffer)  
• Commonly used between parent-child processes  
Process A  
Process B  
write-end  
read-end  
PIPE BUFFER  
Anonymous Pipes Features  
• Not associated with a file name  
• Only work between related processes(parent-child or siblings created via fork())  
• Automatically destroyed when both ends close  
• Provide half-duplex communication (one-way)  
Anonymous pipes enable simple, fast IPC within a family of processes.  
pipe() System Call  
• int pipe(int fd[2]);  
• Common errors:  
• EMFILE: Too many file descriptors  
• ENFILE: System FD limit reached  
• Returns:  
• fd[0]: Read end  
• fd[1]: Write end  
• On success: returns 0  
• On failure: returns -1, sets errno  
Process A  
Process B  
write-end (fd[1])  
(fd[0]) read-end  
PIPE BUFFER  
Pipe Lifecycle  
Parent creates pipe  
using pipe(fd)  
Parent calls fork()  
Both parent and child  
now share fd[0] and  
fd[1]  
Writer closes read end  
Reader closes write end  
Close unused ends  
Data written to fd[1] is  
read from fd[0]  
Example 1: Parent Writes, Child Reads  
(1/2)  
#include <stdio.h>  
#include <unistd.h>  
#include <stdlib.h>  
#include <string.h>  
#include <sys/wait.h>  
int main(void)  
{
int pipefd[2]; // pipefd[0] = read-end, pipefd[1] =  
write-end  
pid_t pid;  
const char *msg = "Hello from parent!\n";  
if (pipe(pipefd) == -1) {  
perror("pipe");  
exit(EXIT_FAILURE);  
}
else { // ---------- PARENT ----------  
close(pipefd[0]); // close unused read end  
Example 1:  
(2/2)  
ssize_t len = strlen(msg);  
if (write(pipefd[1], msg, len) != len) {  
perror("write");  
pid = fork();  
if (pid == 0) { // ---------- CHILD ----------  
close(pipefd[1]); // close unused write end  
exit(EXIT_FAILURE);  
}
char buffer[128];  
close(pipefd[1]); // signal EOF to child  
ssize_t nbytes = read(pipefd[0], buffer,  
sizeof(buffer));  
int status;  
if (nbytes == -1) {  
perror("read");  
waitpid(pid, &status, 0); // optional: reap child  
if (WIFEXITED(status))  
exit(EXIT_FAILURE);  
}
printf("Child exited with status %d\n",  
buffer[nbytes] = '\0’;  
// NUL-terminate for safe printing  
WEXITSTATUS(status));  
else  
printf("Child terminated abnormally");  
}
printf("Child received: %s", buffer);  
close(pipefd[0]); // done reading  
_exit(EXIT_SUCCESS);  
return 0;  
}
}
Example 2: Child Writes, Parent Reads  
(1/2)  
#include <stdio.h>  
#include <unistd.h>  
#include <stdlib.h>  
#include <string.h>  
#include <sys/wait.h>  
int main(void)  
{
int pipefd[2]; // pipefd[0] = read end, pipefd[1] = write end  
pid_t pid;  
const char *msg = "Hello from child!\n";  
/* Create the pipe */  
if (pipe(pipefd) == -1) {  
perror("pipe");  
exit(EXIT_FAILURE);  
}
else { // ---------- PARENT ----------  
close(pipefd[1]); // parent does not write -> close write end  
Example 2:  
(2/2)  
char buffer[128];  
ssize_t n = read(pipefd[0], buffer, sizeof(buffer));  
if (n == -1) {  
perror("read");  
pid = fork();  
exit(EXIT_FAILURE);  
}
if (pid == 0) { // ---------- CHILD ----------  
close(pipefd[0]); // child does not read -> close  
read end  
buffer[n] = '\0'; // NUL-terminate for safe printing  
printf("Parent received: %s", buffer);  
close(pipefd[0]); // done reading  
size_t len = strlen(msg);  
if (write(pipefd[1], msg, len) != (ssize_t)len) {  
int status;  
perror("write");  
waitpid(pid, &status, 0); // reap child  
exit(EXIT_FAILURE);  
}
if (WIFEXITED(status))  
printf("Child exited with status %d\n",  
WEXITSTATUS(status));  
close(pipefd[1]); // send EOF to parent  
else  
_exit(EXIT_SUCCESS);  
}
printf("Child terminated abnormally");  
}
return 0;  
}
Two-way Communication Using Two Pipes  
(1/3)  
#include <stdio.h>  
#include <stdlib.h>  
#include <unistd.h>  
#include <string.h>  
#include <errno.h>  
#include <sys/types.h>  
#include <sys/wait.h>  
#define MSGSIZE 128  
int main(void)  
/* Create both pipes */  
{
if (pipe(pipe_parent_to_child) == -1 ||  
pipe(pipe_child_to_parent) == -1) {  
perror("pipe");  
int pipe_parent_to_child[2];  
// Parent writes, child reads  
int pipe_child_to_parent[2];  
// Child writes, parent reads  
exit(EXIT_FAILURE);  
}
Two-way Communication Using Two Pipes  
(2/3)  
pid_t pid;  
pid = fork();  
if (pid == -1) {  
perror("fork");  
/* Read a message from the parent */  
char buf[MSGSIZE] = {0};  
exit(EXIT_FAILURE);  
}
read(pipe_parent_to_child[0], buf, MSGSIZE - 1);  
printf("Child received: \"%s\"\n", buf);  
/* ------------- CHILD PROCESS ------------- */  
if (pid == 0) {  
/* Prepare a reply */  
/* Close unused ends */  
const char *reply = "Hi parent, message received!";  
write(pipe_child_to_parent[1], reply, strlen(reply) + 1);  
/* Clean up */  
close(pipe_parent_to_child[1]);  
// close write end (parent→child)  
close(pipe_child_to_parent[0]);  
// close read end (child→parent)  
close(pipe_parent_to_child[0]);  
close(pipe_child_to_parent[1]);  
_exit(EXIT_SUCCESS);  
}
Two-way Communication Using Two Pipes  
(3/3)  
/* ------------ PARENT PROCESS ------------ */  
else {  
/* Clean up */  
close(pipe_parent_to_child[1]);  
close(pipe_child_to_parent[0]);  
/* Close unused ends */  
close(pipe_parent_to_child[0]);  
// close read end (parent→child)  
close(pipe_child_to_parent[1]);  
// close write end (child→parent)  
/* Wait for child to exit */  
wait(NULL);  
}
/* Send a message to the child */  
return 0;  
}
const char *msg = "Hello child, this is parent.";  
write(pipe_parent_to_child[1], msg, strlen(msg) + 1);  
/* Receive child's reply */  
char reply_buf[MSGSIZE] = {0};  
read(pipe_child_to_parent[0], reply_buf, MSGSIZE - 1);  
printf("Parent received: \"%s\"\n", reply_buf);  
Blocking Behavior of Pipes  
When read  
blocks:  
When write  
blocks:  
When read  
returns 0:  
When write fails  
with SIGPIPE:  
• Pipe empty  
• Pipe buffer full  
• All write ends  
• All read ends  
closed → writer  
gets SIGPIPE  
closed → EOF  
• Write end still  
open  
Pipe Buffering  
• Pipes use kernel buffer (size ≈ 64 KB on modern Linux)  
• Writes ≤ PIPE_BUF are atomic  
• Good for logging or sending structured messages  
Closing Pipe Ends Correctly  
Golden Rules:  
• Close unused ends immediately after fork  
• Writers must close when done → allows readers to detect EOF  
• Readers must close when done → prevents writers from blocking forever  
Incorrect closure → deadlocks & hangs  
Pipes in Embedded Systems  
Typical use cases:  
• Logging process receiving data via a pipe  
• Sensor poller → filter thread (as separate process)  
• Motor control command-forwarder  
• Inter-process pipelines without using shared memory  
Troubleshooting & Common Errors  
• Deadlock due to not closing unused ends  
• Reader blocked forever (writer didn't close)  
• Writer SIGPIPE (reader closed early)  
• Reading partial messages without framing  
• Forgetting to flush stdout when writing from parent/child  
Summary  
• Pipes: simplest form of IPC  
• Unidirectional & anonymous  
• Use with fork() for parent-child communication  
• Understand blocking, EOF, atomic writes  
• Use two pipes for duplex communication  
Scenario: Parent–Child Line Filter with Two-Way  
Control (1/4)  
Context  
• Build a line filter as a pair of related processes:  
• The parent streams input lines to the child via a pipe;  
• The child transforms lines (e.g., uppercase or ROT13) and sends results back to the parent through a  
second pipe.  
• Parent prints transformed lines and supports a runtime mode switch command.  
Scenario: Parent–Child Line Filter with Two-Way  
Control (2/4)  
PART 1 — Plumbing & Direction  
• Create two pipes:  
• p2c: parent → child (write at parent, read at child)  
• c2p: child → parent (write at child, read at parent)  
• fork(); close unused ends properly in each process.  
Scenario: Parent–Child Line Filter with Two-Way  
Control (3/4)  
PART 2 — Transform Logic (Child)  
• Default transform: uppercase.  
• Read a line from p2c, transform, write to c2p.  
• Exit on EOF when parent closes its write end.  
Scenario: Parent–Child Line Filter with Two-Way  
Control (4/4)  
PART 3 — Control Channel (Parent)  
• Parent reads stdin:  
• Normal lines → send to child, read transformed reply, print.  
• Command :mode rot13 → send control token __CMD_MODE_ROT13__ to child; child switches mode.  
• Command :quit → close p2c write end; read remaining output; exit.
